title: Carreg Goch (Wellington R1465) Bomber Memorial
location_name: Carreg Goch (Wellington R1465), Brecon Beacons (Bannau Brycheiniog)
memorial_type: Memorial cairn with two plaques; wreckage strewn around site; poppy wreaths typically present
narrative: In the early hours of 6 July 1942, an all-Canadian crew from 22 OTU at Wellesbourne Mountford were on a night cross-country navigation exercise when they encountered heavy low cloud over the Brecon Beacons. Flight Sergeant Kemp, the 21-year-old pilot, decided to descend from his cruising altitude of 10,000 feet to try to fix the aircraft's position visually. During the descent, the Wellington struck the summit of Waun Rydd at approximately 2,500 feet. All five crew were killed instantly. The crew ranged in age from 21 to 30 -- Sergeant Yuill, the oldest at 30, had been an observer (navigator) alongside the younger Mittell. All five Canadians were buried together at Hereford Cemetery, the nearest Commonwealth War Graves site, in adjacent graves in Plot A. The crew's deaths came just two weeks after the RAF's devastating "Thousand Bomber Raids," which had placed enormous pressure on OTUs to accelerate crew training.

crew.name: John Beverley Kemp
crew.burial_location: Hereford Cemetery, Plot A (C of E), Grave 5619

cause_of_loss: Pilot descended from 10,000 ft cruising altitude in heavy cloud to fix position; Wellington struck summit of Waun Rydd
memorial_details: The memorial cairn on Waun Rydd is widely recognised as **the highest-situated war memorial in the United Kingdom**, standing at approximately 2,500 feet. It was built in 1980 by the Tredegar Comprehensive School's Outdoor Activities Group, who carried the materials up the mountain. The cairn bears a plaque listing the five crew names and is sometimes adorned with poppy wreaths, especially around Remembrance Sunday. Wreckage from the Wellington is still visible around the crash site. The memorial is accessible via a hill walk of approximately 2-3 hours from Talybont-on-Usk or via the Blaen-y-Glyn waterfalls trail (approximately 8 km loop, graded moderate). The site is a popular hiking destination.

external_narratives.summary: Detailed hiking guide to the Wellington R1465 crash site on Waun Rydd in the Brecon Beacons. Describes the memorial cairn, visible wreckage scattered around the site, and the walking routes to reach the highest-situated war memorial in the UK.
key_details: Cairn described as highest war memorial in UK

external_narratives.summary: Walking guide covering the Wellington R1465 crash site and memorial cairn. Provides route details and photographs of the wreckage and cairn at approximately 2,500 feet elevation.
Accident record for Wellington Mk IC R1465 on 6 July 1942. Documents the crash into Waun Rydd during a night cross-country exercise from 22 OTU Wellesbourne Mountford.
Feature article on Brecon Beacons aircraft crash sites including the Wellington R1465 site. Places the crash in the wider context of multiple WW2 aircraft losses in the Beacons area.
key_details: Part of broader Brecon Beacons crash sites coverage
Multiple crash sites in the area noted
